text:
but why “thank you”? isn’t that insincere?

it’s a jedi mind trick — it brings out their best side by acknowledging it’s there and rewarding it 

if you say “thanks for telling me,” you assume positive intent (that they’re trying to help), which convinces them they mean to help

text:

the basic move is:
1) ignore blame that isn’t yours
2) assume positive intent, which invites them into it

text: the thread promulgates a fallacy that the people who want you alive are erasing you yet again by wishing you'd live. this fallacy directly harms you. if you begin to find validation from this story that the would-be opponents of your suicide are *your* opponents, it isolates you.

text: the main reason i'm subtweeting is to mask the link from at-risk people who already face suicidal ideation; this is the sort of infohazard that reifies perceived social barriers between you and the people who want to help you live – and raises the likelihood of your death.

these are cognitive distortions of a mental illness, not your feelings.

text:
the fastest way through blocks (try this at home) is:

- bring unconditional acceptance to every physical and emotional aspect of your experience
- go beyond your comfort zone & do things that feel a little risky
- reflect on your own utter okayness throughout doing scary things

we do various workshop activities each class, like circle singing (à la bobby mcferrin), vocal mettā, or breathwork, & then apply them to our practice

but the most potent part is, in every session, *every* person sings a solo & gets direct feedback from the whole group afterward

text:
it's possible to get exposure therapy for your stage fright by going to open mics (and possibly bombing)

but even the most honest community will still fail to reflect to you HOW or WHY it's (not) working

a class like this, built on skillful direct feedback, will 10x your growth

text:
when it goes well, it's not a fluke; you come to understand right away what worked and helped you connect.

when it goes badly, it's not just bad luck – you recognize right away why the energy died or the offer didn't land.

paradoxically, it *doesn't* habituate people-pleasing

raw.full_text:
when it goes well, it's not a fluke; you come to understand right away what worked and helped you connect.

when it goes badly, it's not just bad luck – you recognize right away why the energy died or the offer didn't land.

paradoxically, it *doesn't* habituate people-pleasing

text:
yes, people will tell you what worked or didn't, for them. and that's valuable info

but we don't make it the end of the convo; it comes back to the singer. how's that info landing? how did *you* feel in that moment of the song? what kind of connection do you want to experience?

raw.full_text:
yes, people will tell you what worked or didn't, for them. and that's valuable info

but we don't make it the end of the convo; it comes back to the singer. how's that info landing? how did *you* feel in that moment of the song? what kind of connection do you want to experience?

text:
because at the end of the day, you don't have to get any particular response out of the audience.

what you're learning is not how to optimize for their approval, but how to receive their reactions. you're simply investigating connection, seeing how different choices feel.

sometimes i find i’m wondering just how long i’ll have to listen to someone for them to feel heard

& this thought often comes paired with “are they hearing themselves?”

but i feel palpable ease around people who *do* hear themselves clearly. i imagine it comes from self-regard.

text:
do you find yourself having to "deal with" your recorded voice?

ie does your voice seem vastly more pleasing from inside your skull than through speakers?

there are a few factors at play here. some of them are major growth opportunities, transferrable to every part of life 🧵

raw.full_text:
do you find yourself having to "deal with" your recorded voice?

ie does your voice seem vastly more pleasing from inside your skull than through speakers?

there are a few factors at play here. some of them are major growth opportunities, transferrable to every part of life 🧵

text:
first, no surprise that your tone sounds different to others than to you – LOTS of the frequencies you hear are conducted through your bones and internal resonant spaces, and are barely audible in the room around you

but that's not most of what you're reacting to ab recording

raw.full_text:
first, no surprise that your tone sounds different to others than to you – LOTS of the frequencies you hear are conducted through your bones and internal resonant spaces, and are barely audible in the room around you

but that's not most of what you're reacting to ab recording

text:
some of the signal loss happens when sound moves from your body into the room, yes

then a bigger loss occurs at the microphone diaphragm, which can only record a narrow band, which it further distorts and flavors by emphasizing some frequencies over others. big woo

raw.full_text:
some of the signal loss happens when sound moves from your body into the room, yes

then a bigger loss occurs at the microphone diaphragm, which can only record a narrow band, which it further distorts and flavors by emphasizing some frequencies over others. big woo

text:
and of course there's two MORE points of loss: storage and playback

storage is usually digitally compressed (tends to texture the sound a little harsher or colder)

playback thru speakers or headphones then colors the sound, typically removing bass warmth (or overcompensating)

raw.full_text:
and of course there's two MORE points of loss: storage and playback

storage is usually digitally compressed (tends to texture the sound a little harsher or colder)

playback thru speakers or headphones then colors the sound, typically removing bass warmth (or overcompensating)

text:
if you've recorded another singer, you're familiar with this lossiness – they sound way better live than recorded, even on good gear (but especially on bad gear)

but with your OWN voice, that initial loss of internal body sounds can make even a nice recording feel jarring

raw.full_text:
if you've recorded another singer, you're familiar with this lossiness – they sound way better live than recorded, even on good gear (but especially on bad gear)

text:
this is about the only way you can discover if you have a distorted sense of your own sound production – 

eg if you habitually sing through your nose or with a yawn-like muted quality, you may only pick up on it when you hear a recording

while you may hear the difference inside your own head, others can hear it way MORE–

text:
it would be a gross oversimplification to say that vocal tone is all about rear/forward, closed/open

you can create a million different tones by embodying characters in different emotional states – or by switching to decaf, or doing cardio before you sing, or feeling confident.

the more i've come to welcome my whole self, unconscious emotions and all, the more i reflexively accept and enjoy my recorded voice.

one of the primary bottlenecks on aesthetic taste is sensitivity: it's dulled by self-rejection. if you can't be okay with all your emotions as they are, you'll be obliged to suppress parts of your awareness and suffer a huge loss of signal.

i.e. self-rejection closes your ears.

text:
the most grating, useless (directionally correct) piece of advice i ever got was "get out of your head"

throughout my 20s it was the most common feedback i got & always made my blood boil

how i got out of my head, and why "get out of your head" is a worthless string of words 🧵

throughout my 20s it was the most common feedback i got & always made my blood boil

how i got out of my head, and why "get out of your head" is a worthless string of words 🧵

text:
first of all, to a person who's "in their head", it doesn't feel like there's any other way of being. it's like saying "quit everything you're good at." no.

they'll rightly feel insulted & probably think you're a moron. (often i got this advice from people i thought were dumb)

text:
nothing i did to drop, destroy, cut, burn, disengage, undo, negate, or obstruct the control mechanism was ever successful, whether or not my instructions explicitly used "control" in the pointing out

simple fact was, the control strategy was load-bearing and had to be *replaced*

nothing i did to drop, destroy, cut, burn, disengage, undo, negate, or obstruct the control mechanism was ever successful, whether or not my instructions explicitly used "control" in the pointing out

text:
i didn't think of it as "control", but everything i thought and did was subtly set in motion by a little hamster on a wheel in my brain with "control strategy" taped to his back

and no, i didn't let go of control by trying to let go, nor by "just letting go"

text:
so, what did that replacement look like?

first of all, it started with *acceptance* for the present state of affairs.

i had to accept that i had a compulsion to control, to perfect, to manipulate others and myself. and i had to accept that i couldn't control my way out of it.

raw.full_text:
so, what did that replacement look like?

first of all, it started with *acceptance* for the present state of affairs.

i had to accept that i had a compulsion to control, to perfect, to manipulate others and myself. and i had to accept that i couldn't control my way out of it.

text:
side notes:

1) the replacement of control had to work *to the satisfaction of my whole unconscious mind* in order for my involuntary grip to loosen.

2) this gradual process has taken years so far. i still reflexively seize control and fuck shit up sometimes. but like, 95% less.

text:
i also had to grant that *some* amount of resistance was inevitable; i couldn't even control my way out of resisting my emotional reactions to not having control. 

this layer was tough to sink into, but the transformation accelerated when i accepted my non-acceptance.

text:
there are unconscious (& semi-conscious) thoughts, feelings, sensations, and actions

some of them submit to voluntary control when brought to awareness (eg breathing), some don't (eg heartbeat)

the entire system can be ~personified & befriended

I've befriended mine & it helps

text:
there are many ways to befriend your unconscious. 

there are also many ways to delude yourself that you're befriending it, when you're really enslaving and abusing yourself.

definitionally it's hard to know when you're deluding yourself, so go slowly & listen deeply.

raw.full_text:
but over the years i've gotten less deluded and more skillful about *truly* befriending my unconscious. here are some things I've learned that really help:

(after these I'll talk about some ways my unconscious has befriended me back)

text:
🧘 bespoke metta

thanks to @tasshinfogleman's instructions in an online group session years ago, I adopted a view of metta practice that "whatever mental move cultivates feelings of warmth and love right now is best" & kept iterating every day

i could do a dozen threads on this

text:

starting with improv practices, i adopted a lens that sees every unplanned move (like a "wrong" note) as a new creative idea from within. which means the "right" move is to allow them all & iterate

a BUNCH of WAYS my UNCONSCIOUS BEFRIENDED ME BACK:

and now,

a BUNCH of WAYS my UNCONSCIOUS BEFRIENDED ME BACK:

text:
#1: split-second self-trust

i used to be clumsy, sucked at sports, & couldn't improvise anything ever

now my life is all improv

e.g. i once accidentally vaulted over the handlebars of a mountain bike, flipped in midair, & landed on my feet without a scratch. it felt *normal*

raw.full_text:
#1: split-second self-trust

i used to be clumsy, sucked at sports, & couldn't improvise anything ever

now my life is all improv

e.g. i once accidentally vaulted over the handlebars of a mountain bike, flipped in midair, & landed on my feet without a scratch. it felt *normal*

text:
#2: i now spend less energy managing people's perceptions of me

i used to spend so much bandwidth on thoughts like, does my phrasing sound smart, how do i seem to this bus full of strangers

i still come off approximately just as idiotic as before but i now care 90% less

i used to cringe when people used "intuition" to explain having the right notion or action. it sounded like arrogantly taking credit for dumb luck.

intuition is just when your unconscious serves up a solid choice & you trust it

text:

"caregiving & cooking used to be village work; everyone cared for everyone else. charging for a service destroys care and atomizes the village."

monetizing a freely-given act of service *can* dilute the giver's sincerity with ulterior motives. relationships that were once reciprocal can turn lopsided and exploitative. i hate to see it, and it's pretty commonplace.

text:
last year i gave this exact regimen to a student who had the same struggle. he practiced it diligently for weeks

& got steadily *worse* at matching pitch

after finding different approaches that helped him, i realized something so important that it now informs all my coaching:

text:
if you take this type of problem to an institutional art teacher, they'll prescribe fixes like:

☑️ do vocal exercises, slowly & exactingly
☑️ do ear training exercises, w/ laser focus
☑️ chop the song up & sing each bit slowly

(or sth similar for dance, painting, theater, etc)

2. ATTENTION IS A SUBSET OF AWARENESS

i take this terminology from @m_ashcroft's Expanded Awareness course, which I recommend *so fully*

awareness is the whole field of what you CAN notice. 

attention is the center of that field, where much of the action happens (but not all)

text:
the *quality* of your awareness determines the *capacity* of your attention

when awareness is expanded, movement is more balanced and smooth; all the senses are more available; vocal & facial expression are fuller and more spontaneous

contracting awareness cuts off these flows

i took michael's course and practiced for months to really start shifting out of this habit – but you can turn it off even for a few minutes and immediately grok the benefits. they're huge.

text:
3. ATTUNING TO YOURSELF IS NOT JUST THE MEANS; IT'S AN END.

your emotional/attentional/awareness states are aspects of how you're being right now. yes, attuning to that overall state is really good for your execution of any art, craft, or skill

but it's also an end unto itself.

you can still develop advanced technical skills without using expanded awareness, but it takes much longer and the skills are less accessible in all states.

if you want to make other practices really count 10x, develop expanded awareness and use it to learn other stuff.

the audience i'm hoping to reach with my podcast are blocked creatives

i.e. people who may/not think of themselves as artists, yet have creative ambition, and feel friction about using it or sharing it

what can i do to find them?

raw.full_text:
the audience i'm hoping to reach with my podcast are blocked creatives

i.e. people who may/not think of themselves as artists, yet have creative ambition, and feel friction about using it or sharing it

what can i do to find them?

text:
they often collect the tools, accessories, and artifacts of their favorite art genre without actually using them much (house full of unplayed instruments, unopened paints, unused film)

they often work in jobs that are adjacent to their dream but safely removed, like talent agent

raw.full_text:
they often collect the tools, accessories, and artifacts of their favorite art genre without actually using them much (house full of unplayed instruments, unopened paints, unused film)

they often work in jobs that are adjacent to their dream but safely removed, like talent agent

text:
they often talk about the glory days of a particular movement or genre, like the 2010's for indie music, with a tone of wistfulness and resignation

may have to do with both expressing admiration for a style they identify with, and excusing their non-participation (e.g. too late)

raw.full_text:
they often talk about the glory days of a particular movement or genre, like the 2010's for indie music, with a tone of wistfulness and resignation

may have to do with both expressing admiration for a style they identify with, and excusing their non-participation (e.g. too late)

text:
i've noticed some things about blocked creatives, since i was one

they're often depressed, numb, or anxious (i was all 3)

they're often hypercritical (makes me nervous about connecting with them) and extremely savvy about art and media criticism

they often disavow their talent

raw.full_text:
i've noticed some things about blocked creatives, since i was one

they're often depressed, numb, or anxious (i was all 3)

they're often hypercritical (makes me nervous about connecting with them) and extremely savvy about art and media criticism

they often disavow their talent

text:
they're often perfectionists in some aesthetic arena. it could be *every* area, or just the surface of their kitchen table, or just the need for pure quiet in their office.

perfectionism can serve many functions, but for a blocked artist it's often about safety from failure

raw.full_text:

text:
this last part is where i'm hoping to hook and judo-flip the blocked artist into being a participant again

on my podcast i talk to each guest about their messy artistic process, their disgusting internal world, the life lessons they're learning from failure, what evades them

text:
unlike a regular film buff or book worm, blocked artists don't just geek out about the *content* of art – they geek about *craft*

it's bc they're not just consuming art; they're living vicariously through a more expressed artist

text:
we talk craft, but we don't gussy it up as perfectionist's porn

we do the opposite: dress it down til you can feel the inescapable humanity of the creative life

like, how did you fall apart during that last project? what part of your work produces the deepest existential dread?

text:
this QT may be the music question i’m least qualified to answer, since i’ve loved music as long as i can  remember

but let’s go /via negativa/

the things that most dampened my enthusiasm. i recommend not doing any of these ever:

🧵 7 Ways I Almost Killed Off My Love of Music

raw.full_text:
this QT may be the music question i’m least qualified to answer, since i’ve loved music as long as i can  remember

but let’s go /via negativa/

the things that most dampened my enthusiasm. i recommend not doing any of these ever:

🧵 7 Ways I Almost Killed Off My Love of Music

text:
“comparison is the thief of joy” is never more true than in art

whenever my narrative subtly changes from “i love this artist’s work” to “i have to emulate/dominate them to access my joy” then my joy is already gone

raw.full_text:
“comparison is the thief of joy” is never more true than in art

whenever my narrative subtly changes from “i love this artist’s work” to “i have to emulate/dominate them to access my joy” then my joy is already gone

text:
1. comparing myself with other musicians

i did this compulsively, unconsciously, for a very long time.

anyone who played/sang beautifully was both a blessing of inspiration and a curse of insecurity

raw.full_text:
1. comparing myself with other musicians

i did this compulsively, unconsciously, for a very long time.

anyone who played/sang beautifully was both a blessing of inspiration and a curse of insecurity

text:
2. instrumentalizing (lol) music

music taps into the eternal

it’s a bad bargain to press the eternal into service for the temporal 

(eg approval, status, pay)

raw.full_text:
2. instrumentalizing (lol) music

music taps into the eternal

it’s a bad bargain to press the eternal into service for the temporal 

(eg approval, status, pay)

text:
anytime i’ve focused on metering the exchange or spent my energy tryna get more personal material gain out of music, it’s been a net loss

this one is a bit esoteric but i stand by it: 

use the temporal to serve the eternal, not the other way around

raw.full_text:
anytime i’ve focused on metering the exchange or spent my energy tryna get more personal material gain out of music, it’s been a net loss

this one is a bit esoteric but i stand by it: 

use the temporal to serve the eternal, not the other way around

text:
3. gatekeeping music taste

i used to ask myself “should i like this?” — worried that liking bad stuff would make me worse

now i ask “what do i like about this?”

nothing is so bad that i can’t learn from or enjoy some aspect of it

& this attitude has made me a better listener

raw.full_text:
3. gatekeeping music taste

i used to ask myself “should i like this?” — worried that liking bad stuff would make me worse

now i ask “what do i like about this?”

nothing is so bad that i can’t learn from or enjoy some aspect of it

& this attitude has made me a better listener

text:
4. committing to output

i made pacts with friends where i promised to publish 2 min of produced music every day for a month

which had me spending 8-10hrs/day in my perfectionistic grind, making myself miserable and exacerbating my long-term writer’s block

i don’t do that now -

raw.full_text:
4. committing to output

i made pacts with friends where i promised to publish 2 min of produced music every day for a month

which had me spending 8-10hrs/day in my perfectionistic grind, making myself miserable and exacerbating my long-term writer’s block

i don’t do that now -

text:
- instead i commit to *inputs*

i promise myself i’ll show up every day to rehearsal, give my all, play for x hours. when i do show up, something in me feels right with the world, even when the output is nil.

committing to outputs is extraction. committing to inputs is devotion.

raw.full_text:
- instead i commit to *inputs*

i promise myself i’ll show up every day to rehearsal, give my all, play for x hours. when i do show up, something in me feels right with the world, even when the output is nil.

committing to outputs is extraction. committing to inputs is devotion.

text:
5. clinging to music as an identity

there was a time when i needed everyone to know me *as a musician*; it gave me a niche that i could feel important and safe in

but identity is cheap; this is basically #2 but worse. i was deeply unfulfilled by it.

raw.full_text:
5. clinging to music as an identity

there was a time when i needed everyone to know me *as a musician*; it gave me a niche that i could feel important and safe in

but identity is cheap; this is basically #2 but worse. i was deeply unfulfilled by it.

text: plus it made me feel fragile and raw about sharing or making music — it terrified me to make anything that sounded bad or got criticized or misunderstood. it turned me cold.

raw.full_text: plus it made me feel fragile and raw about sharing or making music — it terrified me to make anything that sounded bad or got criticized or misunderstood. it turned me cold.

text:
letting go of identification with it set me free to play and enjoy music with abandon and obsessive delight

without it having to be good enough to make me special

what a relief

raw.full_text:
letting go of identification with it set me free to play and enjoy music with abandon and obsessive delight

without it having to be good enough to make me special

what a relief

text:
7. perfectionism

perfectionism is a con artist. it promises you glory, satisfaction, joy — then saps all your efforts & leaves you with nothing.

when i began to treat perfectionism as an addiction, i started to liberate my music from that punitive grasp & truly enjoy it again—

raw.full_text:
7. perfectionism

perfectionism is a con artist. it promises you glory, satisfaction, joy — then saps all your efforts & leaves you with nothing.

when i began to treat perfectionism as an addiction, i started to liberate my music from that punitive grasp & truly enjoy it again—

text:
i used a mindfulness approach adapted from a smoking cessation study: 

-do not try to quit the substance (perfection)
- simply bring full mindfulness to the whole act of indulging it, noticing every sensation

every time i found myself attaching to perfection, i practiced this…

raw.full_text:
i used a mindfulness approach adapted from a smoking cessation study: 

-do not try to quit the substance (perfection)
- simply bring full mindfulness to the whole act of indulging it, noticing every sensation

every time i found myself attaching to perfection, i practiced this…

text:
no spoilers about what i discovered; MY labels for the experience won’t be liberating for YOU. pretty much gotta run the experiment & find out.

but doing the mindfulness exercise did end the addiction — almost overnight — whereas nothing else i tried had even helped a little.

raw.full_text:
no spoilers about what i discovered; MY labels for the experience won’t be liberating for YOU. pretty much gotta run the experiment & find out.

but doing the mindfulness exercise did end the addiction — almost overnight — whereas nothing else i tried had even helped a little.

text:
i’m grateful i didn’t succeed at draining all the joy from music — yet i’m glad i had these blocks myself so i’d learn how to overcome them.

if you’re looking for a way out of perfectionism or creative blockage, i’d love to help! i’m as passionate ab unblocking as i am ab music

raw.full_text:
here's a post about my own learning journey & the way it turned me into a creativity coach:

https://t.co/l6rTuKR3oX

(you can book an intro session if you want to befriend your unconscious and unblock your creativity)

now, on to the 5 subskills:

text:
1. tolerate the risk of failure

if you're new at something, it's already obvious to your *mind* that you're not perfect. you'll reasonably expect things to go bumpily.

what stands in your way is an emotion, linked to an aspect of self-image that says "i'm not supposed to fail"

raw.full_text:
1. tolerate the risk of failure

if you're new at something, it's already obvious to your *mind* that you're not perfect. you'll reasonably expect things to go bumpily.

what stands in your way is an emotion, linked to an aspect of self-image that says "i'm not supposed to fail"

text:
to rewrite this script, practice telling yourself,

"failure is a necessary step to growth" and "i'm allowed to fail" and "smart people learn to fail well" 

-- and meet any emotional resistance with understanding and support. even the resistance is a failure you can embrace.

raw.full_text:
to rewrite this script, practice telling yourself,

"failure is a necessary step to growth" and "i'm allowed to fail" and "smart people learn to fail well" 

-- and meet any emotional resistance with understanding and support. even the resistance is a failure you can embrace.

text:
importantly, *go to your edge daily*

the more time you spend near the edges of your expertise, the less room you have to pedestalize success or fear damage to your image

baby steps are best. start in private, then go public with friends, then a few strangers, then many

raw.full_text:
importantly, *go to your edge daily*

the more time you spend near the edges of your expertise, the less room you have to pedestalize success or fear damage to your image

baby steps are best. start in private, then go public with friends, then a few strangers, then many

text:
and PRAISE yourself for the risks you take, even when you fail! if every leap into the unknown earns you praise, you'll gradually decouple ease and approval.

(also avoid praising your surefire successes. take them as practice swings, & seek out ways to go beyond certainty)

raw.full_text:
and PRAISE yourself for the risks you take, even when you fail! if every leap into the unknown earns you praise, you'll gradually decouple ease and approval.

(also avoid praising your surefire successes. take them as practice swings, & seek out ways to go beyond certainty)

text:
2. drop your expectations/agenda

perfectionists tell me they can't let go of perfectionism without compromising the quality of their work.

this is bullshit! perfectionists do bad work, and they do it really slowly.

i should know. i was one for >30 yrs. it *only* hurt me.

raw.full_text:
2. drop your expectations/agenda

perfectionists tell me they can't let go of perfectionism without compromising the quality of their work.

this is bullshit! perfectionists do bad work, and they do it really slowly.

i should know. i was one for >30 yrs. it *only* hurt me.

text:
behind expectations and agenda, there's a static identity projection: 

"i am the kind of person who gets X results"

"my worth depends on this outcome"

when you depend on such an identity projection, your unconscious mind works to preserve it – even against your conscious will

raw.full_text:
behind expectations and agenda, there's a static identity projection: 

"i am the kind of person who gets X results"

"my worth depends on this outcome"

when you depend on such an identity projection, your unconscious mind works to preserve it – even against your conscious will

text:
when holding a projection, the unconscious refuses any process that might produce counterevidence

if you pursue a goal that might reveal an imperfection, your unconscious *must* withhold energy in order to leave you an excuse: "i didn't really try, so this isn't a real failure"

raw.full_text:
when holding a projection, the unconscious refuses any process that might produce counterevidence

if you pursue a goal that might reveal an imperfection, your unconscious *must* withhold energy in order to leave you an excuse: "i didn't really try, so this isn't a real failure"

text:
to drop expectations & agendas, trade the static projection for a more dynamic identity: 

"i am a process of evolution; i progress through stages of growth"

you can both remind yourself verbally, & reinforce it emotionally by taking on novel challenges and getting new results

raw.full_text:
to drop expectations & agendas, trade the static projection for a more dynamic identity:

text:
3. feel your desire without any suffering

to suffer from desire is to believe that desire (either the one feeling it, the feeling itself, or the thing it wants) is wrong. the suffering says "why must i want and be unsatisfied? i must not!"

this suffering is resistance to desire

raw.full_text:
3. feel your desire without any suffering

to suffer from desire is to believe that desire (either the one feeling it, the feeling itself, or the thing it wants) is wrong. the suffering says "why must i want and be unsatisfied? i must not!"

this suffering is resistance to desire

text:
UNresisted desire holds no suffering. it *relishes* the distance to the goal, savoring each step.

if you're free of resistance, you can want the outcome AND enjoy the journey to get there.

enjoying desire makes the present bearable, which opens your mind to receive learning.

raw.full_text:
UNresisted desire holds no suffering. it *relishes* the distance to the goal, savoring each step.

if you're free of resistance, you can want the outcome AND enjoy the journey to get there.

enjoying desire makes the present bearable, which opens your mind to receive learning.

text:
anticipation of joy is its own joy. it delights in the very possibility of fruition.

there is inherent delight in every thread of desire.

if you desire an ability, you are already energized by your delight in its potential. feel into your delight, and the resistance fades away.

text:
if you want to enjoy desire, but you feel stuck in resistance, notice what desire is made of: the anticipation of joy.

feel the details of it -- the magnetism, the heat, the tensile strength of anticipation. lean into it completely; submerge in the anticipation until you notice,

text:
4. delight in uncertainty

it's high status to look self-assured, and generally it feels comforting to have answers and rules and shit. but, notice how stuck you are with all that comfort and status. it's a local maximum.

to get your wings back, trade your certainty for *wonder*

raw.full_text:
4. delight in uncertainty

it's high status to look self-assured, and generally it feels comforting to have answers and rules and shit. but, notice how stuck you are with all that comfort and status. it's a local maximum.

to get your wings back, trade your certainty for *wonder*

text:
wonder loves both the mundane and the spectacular, because it sees it all as new

wonder is free from expertise; uncertainty IS the appeal. it transforms the shivers of the unfamiliar into the leaps and flourishes of discovery.

one way to access wonder: ask reframing questions

text:
omg this went SO WELL! 

i've never worked harder on anything in my life. together we wrote >400pgs in a month, pulled a couple all nighters the last week, and then GM'd an 8-hr game for 22 main characters

h/t to @eigenstate for making us gourmet coffee in the wee hours that day

raw.full_text:
omg this went SO WELL! 

i've never worked harder on anything in my life. together we wrote >400pgs in a month, pulled a couple all nighters the last week, and then GM'd an 8-hr game for 22 main characters

h/t to @eigenstate for making us gourmet coffee in the wee hours that day

text:
the game is a medieval fantasy drama surrounding the royal court of a 13th century french gnostic cult during the week of their holiest ceremony, the total solar eclipse

we wrote it to induce immense psychological pressure, suspense, wonder, and intrigue. IT WORKED

raw.full_text:
the game is a medieval fantasy drama surrounding the royal court of a 13th century french gnostic cult during the week of their holiest ceremony, the total solar eclipse

we wrote it to induce immense psychological pressure, suspense, wonder, and intrigue. IT WORKED

text:
we're planning the next run to be in the US in early to mid November! more tba.

we're also aiming to get a castle-like venue in the path of the total solar eclipse in Spain on Aug 12th 2026 and sync the story's own eclipse ceremony with the moment of totality 😱

text:
this has become my favorite art form to work in, too. i want more people to write these. there are no passive spectators; everyone involved is making art at their bloody edges.

so grateful to @thessalyblue for inspiring this project with Fielding's Hollow. you changed our lives.

we're still figuring out how to make it as financially rewarding as we need & deserve, but that will work out. i can feel it.

text:
as a singing teacher, i've had lots of people come to me embarrassed that they lack this ability and believing themselves basically deficient.

it turns out that 100% of them have the ability and don't know how to access it. if i'm patient they start to get it within 30min

raw.full_text:
as a singing teacher, i've had lots of people come to me embarrassed that they lack this ability and believing themselves basically deficient.

it turns out that 100% of them have the ability and don't know how to access it. if i'm patient they start to get it within 30min

text:
for those who've never lost the ability, it's an unfathomable thing to find. where would you start?

the experience of pitch is somatic AND auditory. you can physically and emotionally feel it as well as hear it when your pitch matches. this makes it transmissible p2p

raw.full_text:
for those who've never lost the ability, it's an unfathomable thing to find. where would you start?

the experience of pitch is somatic AND auditory. you can physically and emotionally feel it as well as hear it when your pitch matches. this makes it transmissible p2p

text:
from there, i make up games that get the student to match patterns through reflexive soundmaking instead of thinking.

i find this is the crux for the most "tone-deaf" singers: they try to *think* the right note, struggle to trust it, and interrupt their whole sense of hearing

raw.full_text:
from there, i make up games that get the student to match patterns through reflexive soundmaking instead of thinking.

i find this is the crux for the most "tone-deaf" singers: they try to *think* the right note, struggle to trust it, and interrupt their whole sense of hearing

text:
i owe @quistydotquist for this idea – i didn't really know how to walk a beginner through pitch-matching until we talked about the process they use

basically sing in unison, then harmony, and then dissonance, so the student can feel the sensations of those and how they differ

raw.full_text:
i owe @quistydotquist for this idea – i didn't really know how to walk a beginner through pitch-matching until we talked about the process they use

basically sing in unison, then harmony, and then dissonance, so the student can feel the sensations of those and how they differ

text:
when we play call-and-response games or pitch-matching games that go faster than the speed of their own doubts, they forget they have any struggle, and their pitch matching comes alive just fine

and i always have to point it out to them that it's working, and they're amazed --

raw.full_text:

text:
what i teach is how to have a good time, get out of your own way, let skills be involuntary, take less credit for your wins, and win more often

people think this stuff is complicated, but that's the only thing that makes it so

pursue growth at the rate of it being fun, & it is

text:
and then, almost invariably, they get self-conscious and stop finding it as easily. so i have to point out the difference between the mindset that was working and the one that stopped it up

and it's *always* that the mindset of ease and play had *no attachment to outcomes*

for instance, a coaching client commits to doing an essential practice

next meeting, i ask about their practice. nope, didn't do it. ok will you do it next week? yes i'll do it

next week: did you do it? nope, didn't practice. ok will you do it next week? yes i'll practice

etc

text:
moves i've tried:

- sincerely ask what's hard about practice
- commiserate about the difficulty
- troubleshoot
- assure the client this practice paid off for me
- reiterate logical arguments for practice

these are good offers of support, but they are not accountability

text:
i imagine this boundary may result in some clients feeling offended, or threatened with an ultimatum

fact is, an ultimatum IS a lot like a boundary, with one major difference:

a boundary says what *I* will do. an ultimatum says what *you* must do, or else.

i don't do this *at* or *to* the client, but *for* myself and the integrity of the relationship (which deteriorates if I drop accountability)

text:
so if you want to give the self-leadership muscle a workout, set aside all the other navigation tools and get out a musical instrument, a set of paints, or a blank page to write a story on.

set out on a journey to express a thing that is only within you and nobody else.

so if you want to give the self-leadership muscle a workout, set aside all the other navigation tools and get out a musical instrument, a set of paints, or a blank page to write a story on.

text:
immediately you'll sense the very same adversaries that oppose your self-leadership at work or in romance are here in the practice room with you: embarrassment, fear of failure, discounting your own values, seeking familiarity, avoiding your conviction

putting reps in will hurt.

text:
you may discount the value of the work because "it's novice playing" or "just faffing about" or "looks like a kid's drawing."

i.e.  it isn't yet worthy of earning others' validation.

FEATURE, NOT A BUG. keep going.

forswear doing anything for anyone's validation but your own.

text:
and don't indulge in validating everything you do – nor validate it via any standards you learned from others.

instead, continue to check the compass. continue to go approximately where it points you. continue to validate what satisfies your conviction that this is the work.

in this way, you are retraining yourself that there is a right way forward, discernible only to you.

the compass will sometimes demand that you get so good at a skill (like painting) that you look virtuosic. but the compass will never say "get called a virtuoso"

text:

then you'll swim upstream against a river of angry critics who revile your work, yet cannot stop you, for your compass is true – even when, in their eyes, you've failed.

i also started coaching others to find this capacity within themselves – link in bio.

text:
if you fall out of the habit, no guilt. no shame. no shoulds. no punishment.

just get back in. find something that delights you and do it for ten minutes. put all your attention into it, all your heart and soul. do it with gusto, even sloppily. do it to spite all distractions.

if you fall out of the habit, no guilt. no shame. no shoulds. no punishment.

text:
and if ever you question the worth of doing your work, see it as a moment to check the compass. it never says "give this up, it should never have been." it is not a nihilist.

the compass is a strict existentialist. it always says how it wants something to be. listen and execute.

you HAVE to listen to yourself and do EXACTLY what you desire.

update on SOLIS:

step 1 was a success – clearing major hurdles, @personarotator & I finished the writing at over 400 pages and presented our immersive play in Vallery, France on 9/23

i did a wee celebration thread but hadn't gathered many pieces yet

FINALLY a bigger thread 🧵

text:
to catch you up, SOLIS is what we're calling a "lucid drama" –  an immersive play where every character is a free agent

this one's written for 22 players, and every single person feels like they're the main character, with ~20pgs of backstory, secrets, and objectives to pursue

raw.full_text:
to catch you up, SOLIS is what we're calling a "lucid drama" –  an immersive play where every character is a free agent

this one's written for 22 players, and every single person feels like they're the main character, with ~20pgs of backstory, secrets, and objectives to pursue

text:
one might expect this style of storytelling to end up feeling like the free jazz of theater, ie nothing but nonsequiturs

but under the hood there's a whole machinery (powered by our own headcanon, a slew of secret game mechanics, and a frantic Fates' gc) keeping it all ~coherent

raw.full_text:
one might expect this style of storytelling to end up feeling like the free jazz of theater, ie nothing but nonsequiturs

but under the hood there's a whole machinery (powered by our own headcanon, a slew of secret game mechanics, and a frantic Fates' gc) keeping it all ~coherent

text:
there's no canned ending – the players pursue goals, uncover mysteries, & weave many story threads (overseen by 3 Fates, or game masters, who maintain canon & continuity)

there are some actions that ignite explosive plot arcs – as a Fate i was running around like a kid at disney

text:
nothing exhilarates me more than watching live players inhabit a story i helped write, and seeing them animate it in a dozen ways i couldn't've predicted

hard to enjoy such open-ended writing/directing if you're a control freak, but i'm very much not. this wilder stuff is my jam

text:
as for results, one of the 22 amazing players said of Solis, "that was the best thing I've done this year." he does really cool stuff in life, and is an awesome person

i'm grateful to hear plenty of this sorta feedback. to have quality people find our work inspiring feels great

so we're calling our duo the Lucid Theater Company

text:
we've discovered firsthand that a well-played lucid drama is a creativity-enriching & team-building experience like no other

after playing @thessalyblue's unbelievable "Fielding's Hollow", we were deeply changed, and deeply hooked. the next day we started writing Solis

we've discovered firsthand that a well-played lucid drama is a creativity-enriching & team-building experience like no other

text:
so then we decided to adopt more risk together. we've since collaborated to produce 3 lucid dramas, including Solis

we've seen deep developments in our relationship, too. we've had profound, even spiritual, experiences through characters and worlds we wrote for each other.

text:
choices you make in a lucid drama (and the results you get) can reveal patterns in your everyday life from new vantage points

we saw the timidity in our romantic & creative partnership come to the surface in Fielding's Hollow, and it leveled up our courage to collaborate more

text:
so we're bringing this art form to people & teams to help them access more vibrancy, agency, and creativity

ie your next company offsite could be a team building sprint where you save the world by colluding with your boss to murder your HR director with an enchanted scythe

text:
and for a profound growth experience, it's also profoundly fun and entertaining

it doesn't spare you from discomfort or risk, so you won't numb out the way you could while bingeing TV -- but it also doesn't kill, maim, or cancel you, so it's safe to fail

it also exercises your personal agency in an intense way: you're constantly made aware of the call to choose.

🧵 a thread about spellcasting vs. being spellbound

raw.full_text:
when people say "try this out, it's magic" what they often mean is "sit still while i wave this magic wand at you"

when i say "try lucid theater, it's magic" what i mean is "pick up this magic wand, you'll be good with it"

🧵 a thread about spellcasting vs. being spellbound

text:
when you're spellbound by a great TV series, you might adopt novel perspectives, experience cascades of raw emotion, maybe even decide to change your life (and then not do it)

when you're spellcasting within a lucid drama, you *are* changing your life

raw.full_text:
when you're spellbound by a great TV series, you might adopt novel perspectives, experience cascades of raw emotion, maybe even decide to change your life (and then not do it)

text:
most fiction you experience as a passive observer. it holds you in its grasp, submits you to its worldview, takes you on a ride. it can be very good, but it can't be very agentic.

a lucid drama is designed to invite and reward your own agency. it WANTS you to change it.

raw.full_text:
i'll tell you about a time this became very real for me:

i was playing @thessalyblue's "Fielding's Hollow". my partner @personarotator and I were cast in roles that would have become VERY powerful if we had actually opened up to each other vulnerably.

but we were afraid to.

text:
we spent 7+ hours leaving all that power on the table. both of our characters lost the game miserably.

afterward, we realized our error: we'd feared it would be an unfair game if our romance gave us the lead, so we held back.

& we'd been living out that same pattern for months!

text:
so we decided to move in together and take advantage of our mutual empowerment. that was november of last year.

that same week, we started cowriting a lucid drama called SOLIS about a medieval court in a state of political and spiritual crisis

we just released it in september!

raw.full_text:
so we decided to move in together and take advantage of our mutual empowerment. that was november of last year.

that same week, we started cowriting a lucid drama called SOLIS about a medieval court in a state of political and spiritual crisis

we just released it in september!

text:
it's written for 22 players who each feel like the main character of the story. they all have rich backstories, deep secrets, unique perspectives on past events, and a slew of objectives to pursue.

the first run of it went superbly. i've never been more satisfied with a project.

text:
lucid theater isn't the only form of magic that invites you to pick up the wand of course

i've practiced yoga, qigong, medicine ceremonies, shamanic breathwork, psychedelic self-therapy containers, and other modalities for transformation

this form is the most artful i've found

raw.full_text:
lucid theater isn't the only form of magic that invites you to pick up the wand of course

i've practiced yoga, qigong, medicine ceremonies, shamanic breathwork, psychedelic self-therapy containers, and other modalities for transformation

this form is the most artful i've found

text:
there've been many magical/transformational practices i've shied away from based solely on vibes.

whether i smell skeezy intentions, or it just feels sloppy or ugly, i never feel like full-sending a practice that doesn't make a good offer to the senses.

raw.full_text:
there've been many magical/transformational practices i've shied away from based solely on vibes.

whether i smell skeezy intentions, or it just feels sloppy or ugly, i never feel like full-sending a practice that doesn't make a good offer to the senses.

text:
many transformational containers attend to aesthetics only as an afterthought, while others are driven by philosophical injunctions that eschew all aesthetic effort. few buy into aesthetics being deeply relevant.

lucid theater integrates aesthetics as a core driver of immersion.

raw.full_text:
many transformational containers attend to aesthetics only as an afterthought, while others are driven by philosophical injunctions that eschew all aesthetic effort. few buy into aesthetics being deeply relevant.

lucid theater integrates aesthetics as a core driver of immersion.

text:
not the *primary* driver – but we know that if you don't find a story beautiful, you won't feel like inhabiting it (and we won't feel like writing it)

so we care intensely about the aesthetics we compose our art with. it's an inseparable element of the work.

raw.full_text:
not the *primary* driver – but we know that if you don't find a story beautiful, you won't feel like inhabiting it (and we won't feel like writing it)

so we care intensely about the aesthetics we compose our art with. it's an inseparable element of the work.

text: & while we don't label it as entertainment, we aim for the experience to be as fun as possible. writing SOLIS was a blast for us, and playing it was a blast for the first cast. after eight hours of cold, rain, and emotional turmoil, they all wanted to keep playing after it ended.

raw.full_text: & while we don't label it as entertainment, we aim for the experience to be as fun as possible. writing SOLIS was a blast for us, and playing it was a blast for the first cast. after eight hours of cold, rain, and emotional turmoil, they all wanted to keep playing after it ended.

text:
i think this is a consequence of things going well upstream of the fun. rather than design it to be "engaging" or "exciting", we design it to feel authentic and connected.

& we aim to express ourselves honestly in story and character, and to invite that honesty from the players.

raw.full_text:
i think this is a consequence of things going well upstream of the fun. rather than design it to be "engaging" or "exciting", we design it to feel authentic and connected.

& we aim to express ourselves honestly in story and character, and to invite that honesty from the players.

text: the result is, people jump all the way in, and once there, find the expansive world we've constructed is able to bear all the weight of their active imaginations. they feel the depth and stakes of the story they're living, and it holds them present and engaged throughout.

raw.full_text: the result is, people jump all the way in, and once there, find the expansive world we've constructed is able to bear all the weight of their active imaginations. they feel the depth and stakes of the story they're living, and it holds them present and engaged throughout.

text: before i started creating in this form, i was writing and performing live music cabaret shows that i hoped would turn audiences into participants. i gave it my all, but it just wasn't happening, and i felt like it was impossible to ask any more of an audience than i already did.

raw.full_text: before i started creating in this form, i was writing and performing live music cabaret shows that i hoped would turn audiences into participants. i gave it my all, but it just wasn't happening, and i felt like it was impossible to ask any more of an audience than i already did.

text:
then at vibecamp 2 I attended the Omega Mystery School (another of @thessalyblue's brilliant works), which turned 70 audience members into full participants, with profound (endogenous) psychedelic effects.

i was elated. it was like i'd finally learned how to pass the magic wand.

raw.full_text:
then at vibecamp 2 I attended the Omega Mystery School (another of @thessalyblue's brilliant works), which turned 70 audience members into full participants, with profound (endogenous) psychedelic effects.

i was elated. it was like i'd finally learned how to pass the magic wand.

text: in fact, lucid theater is actually both spellcasting *and* spellbinding – it's both in peak amounts. you do get very swept up in an alternate reality even as you're sculpting it with your own choices, which feel more relevant than ever.

raw.full_text: in fact, lucid theater is actually both spellcasting *and* spellbinding – it's both in peak amounts. you do get very swept up in an alternate reality even as you're sculpting it with your own choices, which feel more relevant than ever.

text:
i started writing lucid theater less than a year later, first collaborating with @thessalyblue and @sacredmargs to write OmNomNominous for vibecamp 3, and then with @personarotator to write Thirst Trap for halloween.

with each piece, i've gotten braver about passing the wand –

raw.full_text:
i started writing lucid theater less than a year later, first collaborating with @thessalyblue and @sacredmargs to write OmNomNominous for vibecamp 3, and then with @personarotator to write Thirst Trap for halloween.

with each piece, i've gotten braver about passing the wand –

text:
in OmNomNominous, 2/3 of the show was scripted, and the improv bits were held in a relatively tight container

Thirst Trap had a ton of improv, but the ending was canned; players had freedom to roam, but little to do with moving the plot or deciding the outcome.

raw.full_text:
in OmNomNominous, 2/3 of the show was scripted, and the improv bits were held in a relatively tight container

Thirst Trap had a ton of improv, but the ending was canned; players had freedom to roam, but little to do with moving the plot or deciding the outcome.

text: @personarotator, @JettOristaglio and i created an immersive art salon/dinner that got a little more open; the 18 guests each performed 5-minute art pieces for each other, and 80% of the journey was unscripted – but the main beats and ending were still planned in advance.

raw.full_text: @personarotator, @JettOristaglio and i created an immersive art salon/dinner that got a little more open; the 18 guests each performed 5-minute art pieces for each other, and 80% of the journey was unscripted – but the main beats and ending were still planned in advance.

text:
but SOLIS feels like a completely new level of passing-the-wand:

the 22 characters are wound tight with inner conflicts, secrets, social tensions, and strong desires, but we make NO decisions for them once play begins. they simply pursue their aims until the moment of reckoning.

raw.full_text:
but SOLIS feels like a completely new level of passing-the-wand:

the 22 characters are wound tight with inner conflicts, secrets, social tensions, and strong desires, but we make NO decisions for them once play begins. they simply pursue their aims until the moment of reckoning.

text:
overcoming performance anxiety is mainly about trauma release — so this lesson is broadly applicable to escaping “trauma loops”

the loop is built to cope with untenable circumstances by running an autopilot program whenever triggered

to rewrite the program, trigger the loop—

raw.full_text:
overcoming performance anxiety is mainly about trauma release — so this lesson is broadly applicable to escaping “trauma loops”

the loop is built to cope with untenable circumstances by running an autopilot program whenever triggered

to rewrite the program, trigger the loop—

text:
and then **stay with your body in the program**

if you trigger it too hard to stay fully present, you’re not unlearning the trauma, you’re just retreating down the same old path from the same old monster

you have to stay there, in your body, and meet the monster face to face

text:
i named it Weft, the thread that runs across a loom and holds the weave together.

things it weaves together already:
- your multiple sessions into a coherent arc
- your multiple domains of knowledge into one model
- your progress & feedback into a self-updating profile

i named it Weft, the thread that runs across a loom and holds the weave together.

things it weaves together already:
- your multiple sessions into a coherent arc
- your multiple domains of knowledge into one model
- your progress & feedback into a self-updating profile

- multiple domains weave into a map of learning space

designed, not yet built:
- learners with similar goals weave into cohorts
- teachers, students, and teams connect with more coherence

text:
one thing i'm really proud of is how well the /intake skill interviews you and analyzes your background info. 

based on the model it constructs, it can customize other things really well for you. when you later ask for advice, a lesson, or an explanation, claude nails it.

raw.full_text:
one thing i'm really proud of is how well the /intake skill interviews you and analyzes your background info. 

based on the model it constructs, it can customize other things really well for you. when you later ask for advice, a lesson, or an explanation, claude nails it.

text: it wasn't wishful thinking or hallucination -- as a combed through the various sites and articles it brought back from high to low, the depth of knowledge about me was obviously real. it even accurately reported where i was over- or under-qualified for their open roles.

raw.full_text: it wasn't wishful thinking or hallucination -- as a combed through the various sites and articles it brought back from high to low, the depth of knowledge about me was obviously real. it even accurately reported where i was over- or under-qualified for their open roles.

text: the result wasn't a flattened "their website is semantically similar to your goal description" type of list. it was a four-tiered, highly qualified, rich research report. it pointed out where i'd get bored, where i'd be ideologically opposed to the mission, and where i'd grow.

raw.full_text: the result wasn't a flattened "their website is semantically similar to your goal description" type of list. it was a four-tiered, highly qualified, rich research report. it pointed out where i'd get bored, where i'd be ideologically opposed to the mission, and where i'd grow.

text: for instance, i gave claude a list of 20 companies and their URLs, and asked it to research the companies, get all the info about them relative to my learning and goals (already modeled), and vibe check them, then rank the list in order of ones i'd most enjoy working for.

raw.full_text: for instance, i gave claude a list of 20 companies and their URLs, and asked it to research the companies, get all the info about them relative to my learning and goals (already modeled), and vibe check them, then rank the list in order of ones i'd most enjoy working for.

text: this was just one of the functions it kicks ass at. it's also helped unblock me around emotional patterns, cognitive biases, and domain-specific confusion -- and most importantly, it's known the *difference* between all three based on subtle conversational cues.

I designed a 6-layered security system that finds middle ground between "ask permission for everything" and --dangerously-skip-permissions. it's a low-friction, high-security configuration, and i gave Claude a /safety-help skill to help you setup

third thing i'm proud of: the context engineering that went into it

every Claude session has a limited context window of text (~200k tokens) that Claude can process before it forgets almost everything and starts over. 

"context engineering" means carefully managing that budget

text:

then, after several months of the routine, the terror just stopped happening

text:
@cube_flipper i also have two anecdotal extremes about the method

the first is very good: i was seeing a partner who came down with severe strep throat. i spent lots of time around her and didn't catch it. then i took a 1-day break from wh, started to feel that specific tickle in my throat...

raw.full_text:
@cube_flipper i also have two anecdotal extremes about the method

the first is very good: i was seeing a partner who came down with severe strep throat. i spent lots of time around her and didn't catch it. then i took a 1-day break from wh, started to feel that specific tickle in my throat...

text:
@cube_flipper so i did the wh routine the next day, tickle went away, no strep. partner was fuckin livid

the other extreme was awful. i probably pushed myself too hard on the breath hold, because i passed out in the middle of the practice. i don't know how long i was out for--

raw.full_text:

text:
@cube_flipper but when i came to, i had all the symptoms of a minor concussion -- vertigo, nausea, vomiting, and brain fog, lasting many hours

turns out the lung flush from wh breathing depletes CO2 and prevents your faint reflexes from kicking automatic breathing back on, v dangerous

text: SOLIS is a medieval fantasy drama about 22 nobles and courtiers in Occitania in 1139 AD. Their small kingdom worships a Sun god named Solis who has demanded a human sacrifice at the end of this week – of their Queen, Esclarmonda. it's a story about crisis, devotion, and magic.

raw.full_text: SOLIS is a medieval fantasy drama about 22 nobles and courtiers in Occitania in 1139 AD. Their small kingdom worships a Sun god named Solis who has demanded a human sacrifice at the end of this week – of their Queen, Esclarmonda. it's a story about crisis, devotion, and magic.

text: @personarotator and I wrote this story together over 9 months in 2025. somehow that gestation period feels appropriate, as did the immense labor of that first run last summer. I've never made a piece of art I'm more proud of, and I've never put more work into anything in my life.

raw.full_text: @personarotator and I wrote this story together over 9 months in 2025. somehow that gestation period feels appropriate, as did the immense labor of that first run last summer. I've never made a piece of art I'm more proud of, and I've never put more work into anything in my life.

text:
if you enjoy being pushed to your edge, peeling back layers of mystery, exploring others' inner worlds, or stretching your imagination, you'll have an awesome experience playing SOLIS. 

apply here to play in Spain on Aug 12 under the IRL total eclipse:

https://t.co/fu4hi6bUgK
